title: Three-component superconductivity: the effect of second-order Josephson couplings
authors: Shen-Yi Peng, Ling-Feng Zhang, Xiao Hu
abstract: Recently, a three-component Ginzburg-Landau (GL) model compatible with the 3Q pair-density-wave state has been proposed to explain the fractional quantum magnetic resistance oscillations of period $φ_0/3 = hc/6e$ observed in vanadium-based kagome superconductors. The physics of this model is governed by second-order Josephson-type couplings, which break both time-reversal symmetry and discrete $π$-phase flip symmetry. In this work, we theoretically derive the complete set of ground-state solutions and construct a comprehensive phase diagram in the GL parameter space, characterized by analytically determined phase boundaries. We identify five distinct ground states: an 8-fold degenerate frustrated state and four 4-fold degenerate non-frustrated phase-locked states. Four of these states spontaneously break time-reversal symmetry. Numerical analysis of the collective modes reveals the emergence of a Higgs-Leggett mode unique to the frustrated region, accompanied by mode softening near the phase boundaries. Our findings provide a comprehensive theoretical framework for understanding the multifaceted physics of multicomponent superconductivity.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive theoretical framework for three-component superconductivity, specifically addressing the physics of kagome superconductors. It maps out a complex phase diagram containing frustrated and phase-locked states and predicts a unique hybridized Higgs-Leggett mode as a dynamical signature of the frustrated phase.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the stability, phase structure, and collective excitations of a three-component superconducting system driven by second-order Josephson couplings.
main_result: The authors identified five distinct ground states, including an 8-fold degenerate frustrated state and four 4-fold degenerate phase-locked states, and discovered a unique Higgs-Leggett mode in the frustrated regime.
method: The work uses a Ginzburg-Landau theoretical framework, employing analytical derivations of ground-state solutions, Sylvester's Criterion for stability, and linear response theory for collective mode analysis.
model_or_system: A three-component Ginzburg-Landau model representing the 3Q pair-density-wave state in vanadium-based kagome superconductors, featuring second-order Josephson-type couplings.
key_observables: Collective modes (Higgs-Leggett, Higgs, Leggett, and Goldstone modes), superfluid densities, and fractional magnetic flux quantization (phi_0/3).
important_parameters: Second-order Josephson coupling constants (eta_jk), Ginzburg-Landau coefficients (a_j, b_j), and relative phases (theta_jk).
main_assumptions: The model assumes the physics is dominated by second-order Josephson couplings due to momentum conservation suppressing first-order coupling.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the stability domain in parameter space; Figure 2 plots relative phase solutions; Figure 3 illustrates the topological partition of the frustrated state; Figure 7 shows the evolution of collective mode eigenvalues.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 3Q PDW model, derives ground-state solutions and phase boundaries using GL theory, classifies the resulting phase diagram, and concludes with a numerical analysis of collective excitation spectra.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic stability domain of the three- component superconducting states. The closed, curved blue tetrahedral region in the (η12, η13, η23) parameter space defines the stability domain: the three-component supercon- ducting state is stable within this region, where the matrix T is positive definite (T > 0). The coordinates of its four vertices, V1–V4, are given in Eq. (8).

FIG. 2. Distribution of phase difference solutions in the (θ12, θ13) plane The relative phase solutions (θ12 = θ1 −θ2, θ13 = θ1 −θ3) for the Ginzburg-Landau equations are determined by Eq. (14). For a given set of parameters, there exist a maximum of 24 distinct solutions, categorized into two groups based on their symmetry properties. Sixteen of these solutions (open green circles) are pinned at high- symmetry points (e.g., multiples of π/2), representing the non-frustrated, phase-locked configurations. The remaining eight solutions (solid blue dots) are located at low-symmetry points and constitute the 8-fold degenerate frustrated state. Given a representative solution P defined by the...

FIG. 3. Topological partition of the frustrated state in the (η12, η13, η23) parameter space. Panels (a) and (b) illustrate the (η13, η23) cross-sections for fixed η12 < 0 and η12 > 0, respectively. The solid black ellipse denotes the sta- bility threshold det T = 0, defining the stable domain for the three-component superconducting state. Within this stable domain, the 3D parameter space is partitioned by the con- ditions Ljk = 0 (solid red lines) into eight distinct sectors: the four frustrated sectors (R−−− 1 , R−++ 1 , R+−+ 1 , R++− 1 ) and four non-frustrated sectors (R2–R5). For the frustrated sec- tors, the superscripts denote the signs of (L12, L13, L23). These sectors satisfy the...

FIG. 4. Classification of ground-state configurations. The ground-state solutions are categorized into five distinct regimes (I–V) determined by the Ginzburg-Landau param- eters. The contour plots display the corresponding minimal free-energy landscapes G(θ12, θ13), where the white dots mark the global energy minima. (I) An 8-fold degenerate ground- state (GS) with minima located at general points in the phase plane. (II–V) 4-fold degenerate ground-state configurations where the phase differences are pinned at high-symmetry points. The vector diagrams (upper right) illustrate the rel- ative orientations of the three-component order parameters (ψ1, ψ2, ψ3) for each regime. Note that for...

FIG. 5. Ground-state phase diagram in the (η13, η23) plane. The diagrams are mapped for (a) a fixed repulsive coupling η12 < 0 and (b) a fixed attractive coupling η12 > 0. Regime I corresponds to the 8-fold degenerate frustrated phase, which acts as a transition zone separating four distinct 4-fold degenerate phase-locked regimes (II–V). Representative phase configurations for each regime are shown within the main plots, where ”+sym.” accounts for degenerate states linked by time-reversal and π-phase flip symmetries.
